name: Serket
description: Serket, also known as Selket, Selqet, or Serqet-hetyt, is a prominent goddess in ancient Egyptian religion, primarily associated with protection, healing, and magic. She is most notably recognized as a protector against venomous bites and stings, particularly those of scorpions, which were a significant threat in the Egyptian landscape. Her name, Serket, is etymologically linked to the word for 'to tighten the throat,' possibly referring to her ability to constrict the venom or the breath of the afflicted, thereby neutralizing the poison's effects. Serket's importance extended beyond physical protection. She was also believed to safeguard the deceased, playing a vital role in funerary rituals and the afterlife. As one of the four goddesses who protected the canopic jars containing the organs of the deceased, Serket specifically guarded the jar containing the intestines, further solidifying her association with healing and the preservation of life beyond death. Her imagery often depicts her as a woman with a scorpion on her head, though sometimes she is represented as a scorpion with a woman's head. This depiction underscores her dual nature as both a dangerous creature and a powerful protector.
subtitle: Protector of the Scorpion and the Deceased
symbols: Canopic jar (specifically the one holding the intestines)

keyMyths.title: Serket and the Protection of Horus
keyMyths.description: During Horus's infancy, after the death of his father Osiris, Isis hid him amongst the papyrus reeds of the Nile Delta to protect him from Set, the god of chaos. One day, Horus was stung by a scorpion. Isis, distraught, called upon other deities for help, but it was Serket who ultimately provided the necessary magic to heal Horus. This myth highlights Serket's powerful healing abilities and her role as a protector of vulnerable beings, especially children. This strengthened her association with protection and motherhood.
keyMyths.source: Pinch, Geraldine. *Egyptian Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Goddesses, and Traditions*. Oxford University Press, 2004.

keyMyths.title: Serket as a Guardian of the Canopic Jars
keyMyths.description: In the process of mummification, the internal organs of the deceased were removed and placed in canopic jars. Each jar was protected by one of four goddesses: Isis, Nephthys, Neith, and Serket. Serket was specifically responsible for guarding the jar containing the intestines. This role underscores her protective and regenerative powers, ensuring the deceased's safe passage into the afterlife. Her presence ensured the continued integrity of the body's vital parts.
keyMyths.source: Wilkinson, Richard H. *The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt*. Thames & Hudson, 2003.

keyMyths.title: Serket's Role in the Royal Afterlife
keyMyths.description: Serket was considered a protector of the pharaoh, both in life and death. Her image was often found on royal tombs, particularly protecting the sarcophagus and the canopic chest. Her presence ensured the king's safe transition to the afterlife and his continued power in the realm of the dead. She was also associated with the protection of the sun god Ra during his nightly journey through the underworld, warding off dangerous creatures and ensuring his rebirth at dawn. This solidified her role as a powerful protector of the divine order.
keyMyths.source: Hart, George. *A Dictionary of Egyptian Gods and Goddesses*. Routledge, 1986.
